coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in the graphene quantum dot-based biological and environmental sensors

coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) materials having unique luminescence properties due to quantum confinement and edge effect. Coupled with their large surface area, chemical- and photostabilities, biocompatibility, and low-toxicity, GQDs have enchanted many attentions for fundamental research and applications. In particular, the broad synthesis methods with ease of functionalization and controlled-synthesis as well as large variety of naturally available precursors, have made GQDs to be suitable for the development of advanced, rapid, and versatile optical-based biological and environmental sensors with high sensitivity and selectivity. In this review, we summarize the intrinsic knowledge about GQDs related to what properties make them interesting as nanosensors and further highlight the recent advances in their synthesis methods particularly from naturally available resources. Furthermore, fundamental sensing mechanisms will also be given to provide basic knowledge and a more comprehensive understanding to the readers about GQDs as luminescence-based biological and environmental nanosensors. The current limitations and prospects of GQDs are highlighted as the final conclusion of this review, hoping that the readers from various multidisciplinary natures to contribute their expertise and collaborate not only to improve the currently available GQD-based sensors but also to develop new sensing concepts which have never been realized.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the main topics about GQDs discussed in this review.
Structural difference between GQDs and CDs/CQDs. Structure of (a) GQDs and (b) CDs/CQDs.

Bandgap-controlled toward tunable emission of GQDs. (a) Luminescence mechanism [29] and (b) full-color emission of GQDs [40]. Bandgap-controlled via (c) size effect [30], (d) surface functionalization [31] and (e) heteroatom doping [32].

Syntheses of GQDs. (a) Synthesis approaches of GQDs. Top-down approach for the synthesis of GQDs involving (b) liquid exfoliation of coal [46] and (c) electrochemical exfoliation of coke [47]. Bottom-up approach for the synthesis of GQDs involving hydrothermal of (d) citric acid [48] and (e) glucose [49].
NGQDs syntheses from biomasses. (a) A two-steps synthesis method of NGQDs from alkali lignin [59] and (b) one-step synthesis method of NGQDs from chitosan [60].

Microplasma technologies in GQDs synthesis. (a) A photo of four Ar microplasmas operating in parallel at an electrode gap of 2 mm [65]. (b) Relationship between the plasma size and pressure [66]. (c) Schematic of microplasma electrochemical reactor setup for the synthesis of GQDs [75]. (d) Rational design of NGQDs derived from chitosan with tunable functionalities [77]. (e) The proposed reaction mechanism of NGQDs synthesis from chitosan using microplasma [64]. (f) Illustration of the microplasma array setup and (g) the corresponding optical micrograph of the 3D-printed 9 × 9 microplasma array device [78].

Schematic of GQDs as biosensors. (a) BGQDs for “aggregation-induced” PL enhancement for glucose detection [102]. (b) PL spectra of BGQDs with the addition of glucose at different concentrations [102]. (c) Schematic of the PL quenching mechanism of NGQDs and (d) the corresponding PL spectra of NGQDs with the addition of DA at different concentrations, as well as (e) their corresponding sensing performance [104]. (f) Illustration of pH-regulated PL sensing mechanism of NGQDs for selective detection of FA, DA, and EP [106]. (g) AuNCs-GQDs as intracellular ATP PL-based biosensors [107].

Schematic of GQDs as environmental sensors. (a) Paper-based sensor containing NSGQDs for rapid screening of Hg2+ in wastewater [113]. SGQD test strip for selective 4-NP detection against organic interferences including benzene, TNT, CNB, 2-NP, hexane, and phenol [114]. (c) Embodiment of chitosan-derived NGQDs with controlled functionalities synthesized by microplasma technologies for Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ detections [64]. (d) Illustration of As3+-triggered AIEE of Fe-GQDs [117]. (e) PL spectra of [a] GQDs, [b] GQDs with Hg2+, [c] GQD-Hg2+ with cysteine, [d] GQDs with cysteine. Inset shows the demonstrations of “on-off” when Hg2+ was added to GQDs ( [a] to [b]) and “off-on” when cysteine was added to GQD-Hg2+ ( [b] to [c]) [109]. (f) PL spectra of GQDs with increasing concentrations of Hg2+. Inset 1 shows the gradual PL quenching of GQDs with the presence of Hg2+, while inset 2 and inset 3 show the corresponding sensing performances at lower and higher Hg2+ concentrations, respectively [109]. (g) PL spectra of GQD-Hg2+ with increasing concentrations of cysteine. Inset 1 shows the gradual PL recovery of GQD-Hg2+ with the presence of cysteine, while inset 2 shows the corresponding cysteine sensing performance [109].
GQDs as CL-based sensors for DM detection. Schematic of (a) CL-based detection using PP-GQDs-KMnO4 and (b) PAD-CL probe for DM sensing [132].

GQDs as ECL-based sensors for biological sensing. (a) Schematic illustration of the GQDs preparation and the biosensing mechanism using AuNP-induced ECL quenching [137]. (b) ECL emission spectra of [a] blank, [b] GQDs, [c] GQDs with AuNPs-ssDNA, GQDs with AuNPs-dsDNA [d] without damaging the p53, and damaging the p53 with [e-o] styrene-7,8-oxide, phenylglyoxylic acid, madelic acid, sodium arsenite, toluene, ether, acetone, methanal, ethanal, methanol, and ethanol for 1h [137].

GQDs as ECL-based sensors for environmental sensing. (a) Schematic illustration of ECL mechanism based on GQD/cysteine system [139]. (b) The corresponding ECL sensing performance on Pb2+ detection [139].
The key features for distinguishing between static quenching, dynamic quenching, FRET, PET, and IFE.*

list-item.para: There is no change in the fluorescence lifetime (τ0/τ ∼ 1). τ0 and τ represent the fluorescence lifetime of GQDs with the absence and presence of the analyte, respectively.
There is a change in the absorption spectrum of GQDs due to complex formation.
Temperature-dependent quenching degree. The quenching effect decreases at an elevated temperature.
There is a change in the fluorescence lifetime of GQDs with the absence and presence of the analyte.
There is no change in the absorption spectrum of GQDs because there is no complex formation.
Temperature-dependent quenching degree. However, unlike in static quenching, the quenching effect is enhanced at an elevated temperature.
There is a spectral overlap between the PL emission and absorption spectra of GQDs and analyte, respectively.
There is a fluorescence lifetime decrement of GQDs with the presence of the analyte.
The distance between GQDs and analyte should be in the range of 10 – 100 Å.
The lifetime of GQDs decreases with the presence of the analyte.

Energy gap between the GQDs and the analyte is the driving force for the PET mechanism. For reductive PET, there is an energy gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of analyte and the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) of GQDs. Whereas energy gap exists between the LUMO of GQDs and the HOMO of analyte for oxidative PET
There is no change in the absorption spectrum of GQDs in the presence of the analyte.
There is no change in the fluorescence lifetime of GQDs in the presence of the analyte.
The distance between the GQDs and analyte is >10 nm.

head.ce:title: Recent advances in the graphene quantum dot-based biological and environmental sensors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) materials having unique luminescence properties due to quantum confinement and edge effect. Coupled with their large surface area, chemical- and photostabilities, biocompatibility, and low-toxicity, GQDs have enchanted many attentions for fundamental research and applications. In particular, the broad synthesis methods with ease of functionalization and controlled-synthesis as well as large variety of naturally available precursors, have made GQDs to be suitable for the development of advanced, rapid, and versatile optical-based biological and environmental sensors with high sensitivity and selectivity. In this review, we summarize the intrinsic knowledge about GQDs related to what properties make them interesting as nanosensors and further highlight the recent advances in their synthesis methods particularly from naturally available resources. Furthermore, fundamental sensing mechanisms will also be given to provide basic knowledge and a more comprehensive understanding to the readers about GQDs as luminescence-based biological and environmental nanosensors. The current limitations and prospects of GQDs are highlighted as the final conclusion of this review, hoping that the readers from various multidisciplinary natures to contribute their expertise and collaborate not only to improve the currently available GQD-based sensors but also to develop new sensing concepts which have never been realized.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is one of the most abundant elements with massive significance in both biological and chemical fields. With the attractiveness of nanotechnology in modern society, many chemists, physics, and materials scientists have been seeking to develop carbon-based nanomaterials for vast fundamental and application purposes [1]. Among those carbon nanomaterials families, graphene is particularly attractive due to its extended honeycomb structure [2]. The long conjugated-π structure makes graphene a zero bandgap material having extraordinary electrical properties, thermal conductivity, and large surface area [2,3], captivating the eyes of many researchers after its discovery by Novoselov et al. in 2014 [4]. Interestingly, since graphene exhibits an infinite exciton Bohr radius, spatial confinement of 2D graphene into 0D scale (ca. < 100 nm) opens the bandgap of graphene, yielding unique quantum confinement and edge effect phenomena [5]. This new type of material is called graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and possesses luminescence properties [5,6]. The existence of this new luminescence properties coupled with chemical- and photostabilities, large surface area, biocompatibility and low toxicity, make GQDs useful for optical sensing [7–9], biomedical [10–12], catalysis [13,14], optoelectronics and energy-related applications [15,16]. Importantly, the synthesized GQDs are usually accompanied with the existence of defects in the form of surface functional groups and dopants, which make GQDs to be versatile across diverse application fields and extremely water soluble, despite the argument that the high electrical properties as an intrinsic merit from graphene are compromised. This high water solubility coupled with the availability of multiple active sites are two important key features sought especially for sensing applications that mostly deal with water media and interaction with various analytes, respectively.

Therein this review article will mainly focus on the properties, synthesis, and optical-based sensing applications of GQDs (Fig. 1 ). We will provide a comprehensive discussion about the most exciting properties of GQDs (Section 2), various synthesis approaches coupled with the currently thriving sustainable synthesis of GQDs from natural resources (Section 3), and optical-based sensing applications of GQDs intended for biological and environmental aspects (Section 4). Finally, we conclude this review in Section 5 provided with our perspectives on where future studies are required to further extend the applications of GQDs across substantial multidisciplinary nature.

Despite their inherent graphitic lattice, GQDs sometimes are still not properly discriminated from their cousins, carbon dots (CDs) and/or carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The most common way to distinguish those 0D nanomaterials is by carefully characterizing their morphological structure. GQDs are typical anisotropic material hybridized by long conjugated π-domain and their size is usually < 100 nm [5]. Thus, GQDs possess high crystallinity due to the sp2 carbon core, which can be perceived from high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Meanwhile, CDs and/or CQDs are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles composed of fully amorphous or a combination of amorphous and crystalline carbon with particle size > 10 nm [15,17]. In other words, GQDs possess higher crystallinity, making their X-ray diffraction (XRD) peak to be relatively sharper than CDs and/or CQDs [18]. Fig. 2 illustrates the structural difference between GQDs and CDs/CQDs.

The resulting GQDs from any kinds of synthesis methods are almost always accompanied by the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups. In this regard, GQDs can also be called graphene oxide QDs (GOQDs) [19], heralding them to be useful directly for certain applications without conducting any additional functionalization processes [20]. Despite that fact, other studies have reported the functionalization of GQDs by surface passivation and/or heteroatom doping to induce new properties required for certain applications with improved performances [21]. Noted that although it is possible to produce pristine GQDs without any functional groups [19], the process is challenging, requires the use of organic solvent, and the produced pristine GQDs have low water dispersion, and rarely useful except for electronic applications [18]. Imperatively, altering the GQDs structures via size modification, surface functionalization, and heteroatom doping can result in different optical and electronic properties that may work well in one application but not for others.

The most intriguing properties of graphene is its extraordinary electronic properties, including ambipolar field effect [4], quantum hall effect [22], and high charge transport mobility [23]. These electronic properties are none arguably resulted from the high quality 2D lattice without any structural defects, enabling the unpaired electrons of π-bonds to have high mobility across the graphene plane. However, reducing the dimension of graphene coupled with introducing functional groups and dopants can alter the electronic properties and bandgap, which in fact is found in GQDs.

An early study by Novoselov and Geim group revealed the effect of size quantization of 2D graphene into 0D GQDs on their electronic properties [5]. When the size was drastically reduced to less than 100 nm, quantum confinement became dominant inducing a transformation from the conventional charge transport mechanism to Dirac billiards. Besides the size effect, the crystallographic orientation of the edges was also found to affect the electronic properties of GQDs. Li et al. studied the influence of different edges (armchair and zigzag) on the electronic and optical properties of GQDs [24]. There was a decrement in the bandgap and binding energy of armchair and zigzag-edged GQDs when the lateral size of GQDs was increased. Nonetheless, despite their larger size than zigzag-edged GQDs, armchair-edged GQDs were found to yield larger bandgap and exciton binding energy. This finding suggests important information that the edge effect plays a vital role in the bandgap broadening of GQDs. A later study conducted by Ritter and Lyding further elaborated the major contribution of edge geometries on the electronic properties of GQDs [25]. In their study, they concluded that zigzag-edged GQDs demonstrated metallic electronic properties and diverged from the energy gap and size relation scaling law due to the observation of a localized state at the zigzag edge which was not present in the armchair state for the same GQDs.

GQDs are excellent photon-harvesting material especially in the short wavelength region due to their strong absorption peak around 200-330 nm of the UV region, which can be associated with the characteristic π-π* transition of aromatic C=C structure. Beyond the π-π* transition, an absorption tail or sometimes shoulder can be observed from the absorption spectrum of GQDs in the range of 270-390 nm due to the n-π* transition of C=O bonds [26,27]. The appearance of this absorption spectrum of GQDs (e.g. peak wavelength, shape, broadness) strongly depends on the GQDs structure, such as size and doping effect.

Other interesting and probably the most exquisite properties of GQDs compared to their predecessors is the optical properties arising from the quantum confinement effect. The electron distribution is significantly modified by the crystal boundary due to the reduced dimension, resulting in a material with a non-zero bandgap [28]. Since the discovery of the luminescence properties of GQDs by Pan et al. in 2010 [6], many studies have been conducted to reveal the luminescence mechanism, yet it still remains under debate until now. The biggest challenge in precisely understanding the luminescence mechanism of GQDs largely lies in the heterogeneity of individual particles from the same synthesis [27]. However, the most agreeable explanation is that the electron from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) will be excited to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) upon receiving excitation energy, then radiative electron-hole recombination occurs, which is known as luminescence phenomenon (Fig. 3 a) [29]. Various excitation energy sources that can be used to realize the luminescence properties of GQDs involve photon (photoluminescence; PL), chemical (chemiluminescence; CL), and a combination of electrical and chemical (electrochemiluminescence; ECL). As shown in Fig. 3b, the emission of GQDs can be controlled to yield various emission colors with specific wavelengths by tuning their bandgap via size modification (Fig. 3c) [30], surface functionalization (Fig. 3d), [31] and heteroatom doping (Fig. 3e) [32]. In addition, the introduction of heteroatom into the graphitic carbon core not only can increase the luminescence quantum yield by suppressing the non-radiative emissive trap, but also can introduce new properties into the GQDs exclusive to the doped heteroatom [21], beneficial for certain applications and even improving their performances.

Generally, GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent and down-conversion emission where emitted light is located at longer wavelength than the excitation light, and the emission is either blue-shifted or red-shifted to shorter or longer excitation wavelength, respectively. This is a common phenomenon observed in GQDs due to the distribution of particle size and functional groups [26]. Wu group reported that the presence of functional groups in GOs could lead to a distribution of Stokes shifts and subsequently generating a broad PL bands that shifted with different excitation wavelengths [33]. Another interesting follow-up study from the same group reported that the excitation-dependent emission of GOs was originated from the “giant red-edge effect” that occurred in polar solvent like water [34]. This giant red-edge effect is expected to also happen in both GQDs and CDs [35]. Meanwhile, Sharma et al. reported that the multiple electronic states of GQDs were induced by various types of particle agglomerate which happened even at dilute solutions [36]. According to various reports, it is also possible for GQDs to exhibit excitation-independent and up-conversion emission [37–39], which can be respectively attributed to the homogeneity of GQD particle size as well as emissive sites [26] and multiphoton active process [38]. Shen et al. suggested another insight that the up-conversion emission could be possibly caused by anti-Stokes PL [37], in which the electrons of the π orbital would simultaneously undergo transitions to higher energy state like LUMO upon excitation with low energy photons, and return to a lower energy state generating the up-conversion phenomenon.

Biocompatibility defined as the ability of a material to perform its intended application without demonstrating any toxic reactions to the surrounding tissue or cell, is indeed one of the most desired properties particularly in biological applications. Substantially, many studies have affirmed that GQDs exhibit better biocompatibility and lower toxicity both in vivo and in vitro compared with other metal-based nanoparticles and even other carbon nanomaterials including GO and CNT [41–45]. Early study by Chong et al. showed that GQDs functionalized with polyethylene glycol (PEG) could be metabolized quickly through the kidney and presented excellent in vivo biocompatibility in mice even after multiple-dosing experiments [41]. Another work conducted by Shang et al. further demonstrated the GQDs uptake by human stem cells via endocytosis mechanism without affecting the cell viability, proliferation, metabolic activity, and any differentiation in the stem cell afterwards [42]. The ease of excretion from the body after in vivo injection is also another biocompatibility feature of GQDs [44]. However, there is still a discrepancy in several reports where some might say GQDs possess excellent biocompatibility but some might also say GQDs still can cause the death of certain cells owing to the generated intracellular reactive oxygen species. Li group compiled the toxicity studies of GQDs in a comprehensive review and further summarized that the toxicity of GQDs was determined by their concentration, synthesis method, particle size, and chemical structure [43].

In general, the syntheses of GQDs are based on either the cutting of bigger graphitized bulk, 2D, and 1D carbon materials (top-down approach) or the assemble of small carbon-containing precursors to construct nanographene domain (bottom-up approach), illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The former usually involves chemical, mechanical, or electrochemical exfoliation of coal, coke, graphite, graphene, carbon nanotube, or carbon fiber; while the latter mainly involves step-wise chemical reaction, carbonization, and pyrolysis of organic, polymers, or aromatic molecules into GQDs. Despite the merit of large-scale production for a top-down approach, the long reaction time coupled with the necessities of strong toxic chemical reducing agents and high temperature have significantly hampered the feasibility of this approach. Moreover, the difficulties in obtaining GQDs with controllable structures and properties further limit the supposedly broad application scope and possibilities of GQDs. On the contrary, bottom-up approach is able to offer GQDs synthesis with controllable structures, properties, and functionalities. With regards to the current energy limitation issue, a sustainable synthesis of GQDs based on naturally available materials like biomasses can also be realized via a bottom-up approach due to high carbon content present in the biomasses. More detailed discussion will be provided below.

The most frequently used method for cutting coal, graphite, graphene, and carbon fiber into GQDs is the liquid exfoliation method which utilizes strong chemical reducing agents. Among those available precursors, coal is considered to be the cheapest and more easily to be cleaved compared to graphite. This is because coal is comprised of nanosized graphitized carbon domains, which are weakly linked by disordered amorphous aliphatic carbon [47,50]. In a typical procedure, the coal was firstly sonicated in a mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid for 2 hours, and followed by heat treatment in an oil bath at 100 – 120°C for 24 hours to produce GQDs [50]. However, with the fact that sulfuric acid has a high boiling point, its removal from the end-product mixture is deemed to be difficult and will consequently increase the production cost. To alleviate this problem, many researchers have moved from acid oxidants to acid-free oxidants by utilizing the solvothermal technique [46,51]. Shin et al. reported a solvothermal technique using oxone oxidant to synthesize GQDs from diverse natural carbon sources [51]. By mixing the carbon precursors with potassium monopersulfate solution (oxone), the generated sulfate and/or hydroxyl radicals can initiate the scission of carbon-carbon bond via redox reaction during the solvothermal treatment. Meanwhile, Zhang et al. irradiated a mixture of anthracite coal with N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) with ultrasonic waves for 2 hours to prepare acid-free coal-derived GQDs, [46] shown in Fig. 4b. The resulting GQDs exhibited two PL emissions at 429 and 469 nm which was plausibly attributed to the different excited states caused by the formation of many edge defects surrounding the nanosized sp2 crystalline carbon structure promoted by the breakage of the coal bridge bond during ultrasound irradiation [46]. He et al. managed to synthesize tunable emission GQDs via electrochemical stripping of coke by modulating the applied current density and methanol-to-water ratio [47]. As depicted in Fig. 4c, The reaction mechanism proposed includes two important key steps, (1) the electrolysis generated OH and O radicals exfoliated the coke edge planes into multilayer graphene sheets; (2) intercalation of SO4 − radicals into the sheets to break the weaker aliphatic sp3 bonds, while the intact aromatic sp2 bonds were integrated into GQDs. Four different emissions GQDs were synthesized, including orange, yellow, green, and blue emission GQDs with maximum emission located at 560, 530, 500, and 440 nm excited under 470, 450, 380, and 340 nm, respectively. Noted that all the different emission GQDs were obtained from the electrolysis of coke, except the blue emission GQDs which were fabricated through chemical reduction of green GQDs. The reduction was carried out in the presence of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) for 4 hours at room temperature [47].

Bottom-up approach produces GQDs from small organic molecules by means of step-wise chemical synthesis, pyrolysis, and carbonization. The typical starting precursor to synthesize GQDs with high quality usually involves polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) which is composed of a single or few benzene rings [52–57], however, the toxicity of PAH has raised many concerns particularly for industrial-scale production. Fortunately, recent studies have shown that a more eco-friendly precursor involving naturally available bioresources can serve as the starting precursor for GQDs synthesis, replacing the previous toxic PAH compounds [48,58-60]. Dong et al. prepared blue luminescent GQDs by pyrolysis of citric acid at 200°C for 30 min (Fig. 4d) [48]. The resulting GQDs exhibited uniform size and surface state which was supported by the occurrence of an excitation-independent emission with the strongest emission was at 460 nm under 362 nm excitation. In 2012, Tang et al. utilized a microwave technology to hydrothermally convert glucose into GQDs [58]. Two years later, the same group introduced ammonia into their system to fabricate NGQDs with various sizes by controlling the reaction time under 280 W microwave power [49]. During the synthesis, the ammonia acted as a catalyst for the intra- and intermolecular dehydration of glucose, and provided N-dopants to the GQDs (Fig. 4e).

Recently a more sustainable way to synthesize GQDs is proposed by the utilization of biomass as the starting precursor [59–61]. Despite being treated as waste in the pulping industries, lignin actually can be regarded as the earth's largest natural aromatic resource and can be exploited as the carbon precursor for GQDs synthesis. Ding et al. fabricated GQDs from alkali lignin in a two-steps procedure [59]. In the typical procedure depicted in Fig. 5 a, the alkali lignin was first ultrasonicated in nitric acid for 12 h to generate lignin segments. It was proposed that during the first oxidized cleavage step, lignin segments with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups were formed by the bond scission of β-O-4 ether linkage in the lignin structure. These nitrogen-containing polyaromatic carbon intermediates were then refused into NGQDs via hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 12 h. The resulting NGQDs exhibited an excitation-dependent emission contributed by the presence of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities on the GQDs, which might be beneficial for multicolored bioimaging applications [62]. A recent study also reported the production of GQDs from biorefinery waste, Miscanthus, comprises of sugars and depolymerized lignin [61].

Meanwhile, chitosan composed of 2-amino-2-deoxy-D-glucopyranose units is one of the most abundant biomass which can be obtained from crustacean shells. Kumar et al. utilized chemical vapor decomposition (CVD) technique to realize a one-step synthesis of NGQDs from chitosan under 250 – 300°C (Fig. 5b) [60]. It was proposed that the high temperature treatment will depolymerize the chitosan chains and decompose the pyranose rings to produce nitrogen-containing compounds (HCN). The HCN would then be adsorbed on the pre-annealed copper foil at 1000°C and nucleated to form polycrystalline NGQDs. Since both lignin and chitosan are long chain biopolymers, the synthesis of either GQDs or NGQDs from these precursors might be considered as the combination of both top-down and bottom-up approaches instead of just a single approach.

Recently, microplasmas have emerged as a promising method for the synthesis of GQDs owing to their environmental compatibility, scalability, and simplicity [63,64]. The key feature of the reactor geometry of microplasmas is the spatial confinement of the plasma to sub-millimeter scales (Fig. 6 a) [65], reducing the breakdown voltage to enable discharge ignition and sustain it even at high pressure. Fig. 6b shows the unique non-equilibrium states at reduced plasma size, where the gas temperature is lower than the electron temperature (Tg < Te ) [66]. As a result, highly reactive species, such as electrons, radicals (e.g. OH˙, O˙, O2¯˙, and NO˙), and ions can be generated at low energy consumption in ambient conditions [67–70], making it suitable for colloidal nanomaterials synthesis (e.g. GQDs and carbon dots (CDs), as well as metal and silicon nanoparticles) [64,71-73]. One example of a direct current (DC) microplasma electrochemical reactor is presented in Fig. 6c. By using this setup, our group has successfully fabricated GQDs from organosulfate and revealed that both the number of carbon chains in organosulfate and discharge polarity would affect the GQDs formation [74–76]. In the latest publications, our group has managed to engineer the bandgap of NGQDs derived from chitosan biomass in one step by simply modulating the liquid chemistry during the plasma synthesis at ambient conditions (Fig. 6d) [77]. The proposed reaction mechanism pathway involved a sequence of bond scission and reconstruction reactions, in which the NGQDs size could be controlled by adjusting the OH· radical concentration at the plasma-liquid interface via pH value (Fig. 6e) [64]. In addition, microplasma technologies can be further developed into a system of microplasma arrays containing up to a hundred of microchannel plasma jets and/or combined with microfluidic devices [78–80]. Sun et al. utilized a 3D printing technology to design a full 9 × 9 array of microchannels with an overall area of 1.25 cm2 (Fig. 6f and g) [78]. Substantially, without the need for sophisticated vacuum equipment, strong reducing agents, and harsh reaction conditions [79], microplasma technologies combined with sustainable bioresources is compliant for the facile and large-scale production of GQDs, alleviating the current shortfalls of GQDs synthesis.

Detection of biological and environmental substances had been being developed since ancient times due to their significance in ensuring the safety of human beings and ecological balance. Although there are already several developed detection methods including high-performance liquid chromatography, atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively-coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and electrochemistry [81,82], they still suffer from cost- and time-inefficiencies, cumbersome specimen preparations and detection procedures, as well as low selectivity and sensitivity [83–85], limiting the practicality for real-time monitoring and even for either in situ or in vivo studies. Nonetheless, the unique optical properties derived from the quantum confinement and edge effect, coupled with the ease of functionalization of GQDs have caused a significant impact particularly in the development of cost-efficient, rapid, and simple nanosensors with high selectivity and sensitivity, outplaying the conventional detection methods. As mentioned before, the general luminescence mechanism of GQDs involves electron excitation from the HOMO to the LUMO, and is subsequently followed by a luminescence phenomenon during the electron-hole recombination process. During the excitation process, various excitation energy sources can be used, thus the implementation of GQDs in luminescence-based sensing can be simply categorized into PL-, CL-, and ECL-based sensing. These three different types of luminescence-based sensing for biological and environmental applications will be the main discussion of this review article that can be seen in the following section.

The utilization of GQDs for PL-based sensing lies in the PL change, either quenching or enhancement, upon interaction with the analytes. Zu et al. have published a review article about the PL quenching mechanisms of CDs which commonly include static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE) [86]. These types of quenching mechanisms can be expected to also occur in GQDs and have been widely used to explain the quenching mechanism in biological and environmental sensing applications [8,9,87].

Static quenching usually occurs due to the complex formation between the GQDs and analyte in the ground state which does not fluoresce even after absorbing the photon. Meanwhile, dynamic quenching can be explained by the deactivation of the excited state, usually by spin exchange, caused by the collision between the GQDs and analyte. FRET is usually caused by the energy transfer from GQDs in the excited state to the analyte in the ground state due to dipole interaction. This mechanism can be perceived from the overlapping absorption and emission spectra of the analyte and GQDs, respectively. In the case of PET, there is an occurrence of electron transfer between the GQD as an electron donor with the analyte as an electron acceptor or vice versa, forming both cation and anion radicals, respectively. The electron donor and electron acceptor form a complex which does not fluoresce upon relaxation to the ground state. PET can be differentiated into reductive and oxidative PET. The former is when GQD as an electron acceptor receives an electron from the analyte (electron donor). On the contrary, oxidative PET is when GQD donates an electron to the analyte (electron acceptor). Although IFE can promote the PL intensity reduction of GQDs, sometimes it can also be regarded as an apparent quenching instead of a quenching process. This is due to the fact that at high analyte concentration in the medium, some of the excitation and/or emitted photons are absorbed by the analyte itself. Hence, IFE usually occurs when there is a spectral overlap between the GQDs excitation spectrum and analyte absorption spectrum in the detection system. For the convenience of the reader, we further summarize the key features in distinguishing the quenching mechanisms of GQDs in Table 1 .

In addition, the PL behavior of GQDs can also be affected by the surrounding medium, including pH and temperature. The temperature-dependent behavior of GQDs is similar to CDs which is generally due to the activation of non-radiative channels such as surface traps or defects at higher temperatures, [88] resulting in PL quenched. On the other hand, the underlining mechanism of pH-dependent PL behavior of GQDs can be reasoned to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups that can alter the Fermi level of GQDs [31,63]. Although both temperature and pH can be considered as physical and chemical parameters affecting the PL behavior of GQDs, a linear relationship between the PL intensity changes with respect to pH value or temperature can still be achieved, enabling the use of GQDs for pH [63,77,89-92] and temperature [64,93,94] nanosensors, which open an avenue for intracellular study [94,95].

Biosensors based on GQDs have been widely used for the efficient detection of biometric components. Biosensors are diagnostic devices that include two components: biometric components (biomolecules like dopamine (DA), nucleic acids, carbohydrates, mRNA, enzymes, DNA, receptors, antibodies) and sensors [96]. The sensor transforms a particular biological event into a quantifiable and easy-to-process signal that is equivalent to the target analyte concentration under certain reaction conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . Biosensing mechanisms cover a variety of approaches, including optical, electrical, and electrochemical mechanisms (Fig. 7). Electrochemical sensors are one of the most widely used systems in the biosensor development owing to their several advantages [97], including low cost, rapidity, simplicity, great miniaturization, high sensitivity, and non-invasive nature. However, the current development of GQD-based electrochemical sensors has been hampered by the problems of low accuracy, selectivity, stability, and sensitivity [98]. Further study is needed to solve the above problems.

Colorimetry-based biosensing technologies can offer lower equipment costs and more simple procedures compared with optical, electrical, and electrochemical methods, yet it is important to note that the possibility of colorimetry for quantitative detection is limited [99]. Quantitative detection is important in biosensing applications, especially to signify the infection severity from virus, which is often found in the lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) technology [100,101]. Despite the merits and importance, the application of LFIA in the biosensing field is hindered by limited sensitivity and accuracy [101]. This review, therefore, will focus on the utilization of the unique luminescence properties of GQDs as optical sensors based on PL, CL, and ECL methods, serving as other alternatives for biological and environmental sensors.

Monitoring glucose concentration in the human body is essential in the management of diabetes and some cancers. Zhang et al. developed a label-free biosensor for glucose detection based on boron-doped GQDs (BGQDs) [102]. This report showed that the reaction between the two boronic acid groups and two cis-diol units of BGQDs and glucose, respectively, promoted the conformational rigidity of the BGQD/glucose clusters. As a consequence, intramolecular rotation was limited, leading to PL enhancement (Fig. 8 a). Additionally, the results further demonstrated that BGQDs might be highly specific only for glucose and not for their isomeric cousins such as fructose, mannose, and galactose, as they could avoid glycans with only one cis-diol unit. A proportional PL intensity increment of BGQDs with respect to glucose concentration was observed in the range of 0.05 – 10 mM, yielding a limit of detection (LoD) of 0.03 mM (Fig. 8b).

Dopamine (DA) is one important catecholamine neurotransmitter that regulates the function of the central nervous and cardiovascular systems. Concentration disorder of DA can lead to several diseases including schizophrenia, Parkinson's disease, and depression [103,104]. Zhao et al. reported the DA sensing by exploiting the noncovalent interaction (i.e. electrostatic interactions, π-π stacking, and hydrogen bonding) between the oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQDs with diols, amine functional groups, and phenyl in DA to promote static PL quenching mechanism of GQDs [103]. The PL of GQDs was linearly quenched with increasing concentration of DA from 0.25 – 50 µM with LoD as low as 90 nM. Recently Ma et al. reported the DA sensing using NGQDs with the quenching performance was induced by the interaction between the pyridinic N and catechol group of NGQD and DA, respectively, (Fig. 8c) [104]. The phenoxy anions of DA in PBS solution were prone to be adsorbed to the pyridinic N site of NGQDs caused by the specific donor-bridge-acceptor structures. and followed by non-fluorescent ground state formation induced by the large delocalized conjugated-π structure. As a consequence, the PL intensity of GQDs was gradually quenched with increasing DA concentrations (Fig. 8d), covering linear detection from 0.01 – 3 µM with LoD of 3.3 nM (Fig. 8e).

In another case, folic acid (FA) is one of the most important vitamins in humans and participates in several metabolic processes. FA concentration disorders may lead to leukopenia, neural tube defect in pregnant women, Alzheimer, anorexia, and gastrointestinal syndrome [105]. Recently, our own group made a breakthrough in the development of PL-based biosensors, where FA, DA, and epinephrine (EP) could be selectively detected with high sensitivity using a single NGQD only via altering the pH value during the detection [106]. Fig. 8f illustrates that the sensing mechanisms were associated with the interaction between the NGQDs and the analytes through enhanced π-π interaction at different pH values. The selective detection covered 0.8 – 80 μM for FA with LoD of 81.7 nM, and 0.4 – 100 for both DA and EP with LoD as low as 57.8 and 16.7 nM, respectively.

Another important biomolecule involves adenosine triphosphate (ATP) which plays important role in cells by providing energy and participating in the metabolism of fats, proteins, sugars, and nucleic acids. By measuring the concentration of intracellular ATP, one can diagnose apoptosis, necrosis, and even cancer. Zhang et al. developed an "off-on" biosensor using GQDs and gold nanocrosses (AuNCs) to sense intracellular ATP concentrations (Fig. 8g) [107]. The PL intensity of GQDs and AuNCs was enhanced by increasing the concentration of ATP through the recognition of the presence of ATP and binding to ATP by the appropriate body. Using this method, a linear concentration range of 0.3 to 2.0 mM with a LoD of 0.27 mM was obtained. The excellent performance of this "off-on" sensor in intracellular ATP detection was further proven by the high selectivity even in the presence of interferences, coupled with the low cytotoxicity. On the other hand, Qu et al. have successfully designed a PL biosensor based on NGQDs for the label-free detection of tyrosinase (TYR) and acid phosphatase (ACP) [108]. As a copper-containing enzyme, TYR catalyzes the hydroxylation of phenolic compounds into catechol derivatives and further oxidizes it into ortho-quinone products. On the other hand, ACP is an everpresent digestive enzyme that catalyzes the release of phosphate groups from the hydrolysis of phosphate monoester during digestion under acidic conditions. The PL intensity of NGQDs showed gradual decrement and increment as the concentration of TYR and ACP increased from 0.43 – 3.85 U/mL and 0.04 – 0.7 mU/mL, respectively, with LoD of 0.15 U/mL for TYR and 0.014 mU/mL for ACP. Importantly, this method could be successfully applied in real sample detections with high selectivity and reproducibility even in the presence of interferences. Table 2 summarizes the recent works about GQDs as PL-based biosensors.

Besides biological sensing, GQDs have also been widely implemented for environmental sensing applications. Environmental contamination especially caused by heavy metal ions such as Hg2+ has raised many concerns in sustaining ecological balance. When water is contaminated, the contaminations can spread into soil and air, thereby disrupting the ecological balance and endangering living beings [110–112]. When Hg2+ is accumulated in the body from contaminated water and food, Hg2+ can cause much damage to the brain, kidney, and nervous system [64]. The well-known Minamata disease in Japan in 1956 was also due to this Hg2+ contamination. Anh et al. synthesized N,S-co-doped GQDs (NSGQDs) for selective Hg2+ detection with a linear range of 0.1 – 15 µM and LoD of 0.14 nM [113]. In the environmental aspect, the versatility of rapid screening has urged many researchers to develop GQD-based test strips to improve the practicality in water quality control. Therefore, to allow rapid screening of real water sample against Hg2+, the same group further fabricated fluorescent paper strips containing NSGQDs, which were still discriminative toward Hg2+ (Fig. 9 a) [113]. Another study also used a similar paper-based strategy to enable rapid screening against 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) which is a recalcitrant organic pollutant [114]. This study employed sulfur-doped GQDs (SGQDs) which could promote π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding with 4-NP, enabling high selectivity for 4-NP against other interferences like benzene, trinitrotoluene (TNT), 1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene (CNB), 2-NP, hexane, and phenol, depicted in Fig. 9b. In addition, GQDs can also be incorporated into a polymeric matrix like aerogel to fabricate a fluorescent nanocomposite system usable for polyaromatic compounds in water [115].

On the other hand, Cu2+ is another type of heavy metal, yet is also an essential element in the human body that plays a significant role in various physiological functions. Therefore, the concentration of Cu2+ needs to be maintained at a physiological level to avoid gastrointestinal disturbance, liver and kidney damage [57,85]. Zhang et al. reported coal-derived GQDs for Cu2+ detection from 0 – 8 µM and LoD of 0.29 µM [46]. The study revealed that the multisite coordination between Cu2+ and the oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs contributed to the PL quenching by forming GQD-Cu2+ non-fluorescent complexes. Besides Cu2+, Fe3+ is also another metal ion that plays a critical role in the human body system by controlling cellular metabolism, oxygen transport, enzyme catalysis, and nucleic acid synthesis [116]. Thus, the concentration of Fe3+ in the human body also needs to be monitored continuously. Wang et al. functionalized GQDs with amino groups to enable improved Fe3+ sensing performance caused by the strong affinity of Fe3+ with the oxygen and nitrogen elements on amino-functionalized GQDs surface. The reported strategy yielded a linear detection range starting from 1 – 50 µM with a LoD of 0.51 nM. As previously discussed, our group has successfully fabricated NGQDs with controllable functionalities using microplasma technologies from single chitosan biomass [64,77]. By using this technique, we were able to produce different types of NGQDs usable for selective detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+, shown in Fig. 9c [64]. The embodiment of functionalities-induced metal ion affinity (O functional group and N-dopant types) allowed the different fabricated NGQDs to be used for Hg2+ and Cu2+ detections in the range of 0.5 – 100 μM with LoD as low as 47.9 nM and 146.5 nM, respectively, and Fe3+ detection in the range of 1 – 300 μM with LoD as low as 66.7 nM. This study offered an insight into the possibilities to engineer the synthesis of NGQDs having specific properties for the desired applications.

Meanwhile, arsenic ion (As3+) is one of the most toxic ions which has caused many concerns in preserving water quality, therefore, the concentration allowed by World Health Organization (WHO) in drinking water is only 10 μg/L [117]. Pathan et al. fabricated Fe-GQDs by combining GQDs with amine-functionalized Fe3O4 with the addition of EDC/NHS as crosslinkers to bind the carboxylic and amine functional groups from GQDs and Fe3O4, respectively [117]. As demonstrated in Fig. 9d, the addition of As3+ to the resulting Fe-GQDs granted aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) phenomenon to the Fe-GQDs, enabling sensitive detection of As3+ from 10 – 100 ppb with LoD of 5.1 ppb. They attributed the AIEE phenomenon to the synergy between the hydroxyl groups and Fe3O4 that assisted the coordination with As3+ through physical and chemical adsorption processes, forming structurally rigid and rotational-free Fe-GQDs and As3+ complexes with enhanced PL behavior.

Another interesting PL-based sensor design using GQDs is by implementing an “off-on” strategy. This work is based on the idea that once the PL of the GQDs has been quenched by metal ions, another analyte is added to the mixture to chelate the metal ions from the GQDs, resulting in the PL recovery. Therefore, simultaneous detections of two different analytes can be realized by using this “off-on” strategy. Li et al. revealed the possibility of simultaneous “off-on” detection of Hg2+ and cysteine using GQDs derived from citric acid (Fig. 9e) [109]. As the concentration of Hg2+ added to the GQDs was increased, charge transfer from GQDs to Hg2+ occurred even more, gradually decreasing the PL of GQDs (Fig. 9f). Inset 2 shown in Fig. 9f reveals a linear detection range from 1 – 50 nM with a LoD of 0.439 nM. In addition, the result also showed another linear relationship at higher Hg2+ concentrations from 120 – 2000 nM (Fig. 9f inset 3). Interestingly, despite giving no response at all to the PL intensity of pristine GQDs (Fig. 9e [d]), the addition of cysteine could recover the quenched PL in the GQD-Hg2+ mixture (Fig. 9e [c] & Fig. 9g). It was believed that the strong interaction between the cysteine thiol (–SH) group and Hg2+ was capable of chelating Hg2+ from GQDs surface, giving a possibility for cysteine sensing in the range of 10 – 600 nM and LoD of 4.5 nM for a mixture of GQDs and 2.5 µM of Hg2+ (Fig. 9g inset 2). There are also other works that report on the utilization of GQDs for other heavy metal ions, rare earth elements, and organic insecticides like Pb2+, Cr6+, Ce4+, and omethoate [118–122]. Table 3 summarizes the recent works about GQDs as PL-based environmental sensors.

CL is a photon emission-based analytical technique used to explain the phenomenon of photon emission as electrons return from an excited state to the ground state during a chemical reaction. CL-based sensing has raised in popularity in sensors owing to its high sensitivity, wide linear range, relatively simple equipment, and no interference from background light scattering caused by the absence of a light source [124–126]. However, additional chemical reagents need to be added to induce some chemical and physical parameters effects, which may cause sensing errors and are not suitable for in vivo purposes [127]. Due to these shortcomings, there are only a few reports of CL-based GQD sensors developed so far, as presented in Table 4 .

Chemical biosensors are like analytical devices that can detect a single target substance or a mixture of target analytes in a solution. Wu's group developed CL-based biosensors for the detection of CA-125, an ovarian cancer biomarker, by using chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) to GQDs [128]. In the typical procedure, GQDs were first immobilized to the silanized glass via electrostatic interaction. Then through amide conjugation, capture antibody (cAb) unique to the CA-125 antigen could be covalently linked to the GQDs, while the unreacted sites on the glass were secured by Bovine serum albumin (BSA). Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) enzyme which was used to catalyze the production of reactive oxygen species from H2O2 that could oxidize luminol to the singlet dianion and generate CL emission, was labelled with an antibody (Ab-HRP). Therefore, in the presence of CA-125 antigen, a complex sandwich structure involving GQDs-cAb + CA-125 + Ab-HRP was formed, making the HRP to be a close proximity with the GQDs that could enable CRET from the singlet dianion to the GQDs. Consequently, the CL intensity was linearly quenched with increasing concentration of CA-125 in the range of 0.1 – 600 U/mL and LoD of 0.05 U/mL for CA-125.

Recently Zhang et al. proposed a possible mechanism of NSGQD -NaHSO3-NaClO CL system for sensitive detection of FA in food samples [129]. The first CL path was caused by the energy transfer from excited state sulfur dioxide (SO2*) to NSGQD due to the reaction of NaHSO3-NaClO. Secondly, the radical (˙OH and ˙O2−) produced in the NSGQD-NaHSO3-NaClO system could react with NSGQD and generate NSGQD˙+ and NSGQD˙−. Then, they would combine with ˙O2− and ˙OH by electron transfer annihilation and form NSGQD*. Lastly, ˙O2− was able to combine with ˙OH and generate singlet oxygen (1O2). After that, 1O2 will react with 1O2 to generate (1O2)2* which could transfer the energy to NSGQD to generate NSGQD*, producing strong emission during electron-hole recombination. This proposed mechanism was usable for FA detection with a linear range of 0.5 – 70 μM and LoD of 78 nM.

Effective sensors can also be designed using GQDs as catalysts in the CL system Shi et al. prepared Cu(II)/Cu2O/NGQDs type nanocomposite materials for catalyzing UA oxidation into allantoin by uricase under H2O2 [130]. This catalysis reaction was usable for sensing uric acid (UA) in serum and urine. During the reaction, reactive oxygen species were generated and reacted with luminol to form an unstable endoperoxide which could be rapidly decomposed into an excited 3-aminophthalate anion that could produce emission when returned to the ground state. The CL intensity showed a linear relationship with UA concentration starting from 0.16 – 4.0 μM and the LoD was 0.041 μM. This chemical biosensor is an attractive candidate for the development of ultra-sensitive CL sensing methods for further applications in the detection of important molecules.

A fascinating study on hypochlorite detection was reported by Shokri's group [131]. The goal of this research was to detect common pollutants (free chlorine) in water. Since this surrogate could directly oxidize GQDs and generate the CL phenomenon, there was no need to add an oxidant to assist in the generation of the CL phenomenon, which was quite friendly to the environment, but in order to further improve the sensing sensitivity, Shokri's group also tried adding a surfactant (cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide; CTAB). The results showed that CTAB could increase the CL intensity by about 18 folds, enhancing its sensitivity significantly. The authors further successfully implemented this CL system for free chlorine determination in spiked tap water and pool water samples, and the results presented effective determination of hypochlorite from 0.5 μM – 1.0 mM with LoD of 0.3 μM.

The enhancement of GQD-KMnO4 CL by polyphosphate (PP) for the determination of deltamethrin (DM) has also been reported recently [132]. DM is a type of chemical often found in pesticides, thus there is a tendency for water and foodstuff contamination by DM, which can further lead to liver dysfunction, protein damage, and genotoxicity induced by the generation of reactive oxygen species. Therefore, Yahyai et al. proposed a mechanism for the reaction of KMnO4 with GQDs to produce a CL phenomenon. The reaction of KMnO4 with GQDs generated excited GQDs (GQDs*) with emission at 490 nm. In addition, they found that PP changed the mechanism of CL, and the main luminescence was shifted from 490 nm to 695 nm due to the change of the main luminophore from GQD to Mn2+ (Fig. 10 a). The experimental results showed that the CL-amplified emission phenomenon caused by the GQDs-KMnO4 system could effectively improve its sensitivity when measuring trace DM. As illustrated in Fig. 10b, the practicality of this work was further improved by employing a facile paper-based analytical device (PADs) for optimizing the sensing performance. A wide linear detection was obtained in the range of 0.3 – 10 μg/mL and a low LoD of 0.15 μg/mL.

ECL is simply known as the conversion of electrical energy into radiative energy by the formation of reactive emissive intermediates through an electron transfer reaction at the electrode surface [135]. Thus, ECL can also be called electrogenerated CL. As the name suggests, this sensing method combines the advantages of electrochemistry and CL techniques, including fast response times, high sensitivity and selectivity, regeneration of ECL emitter, and suppression of self-quenching from side reactions [136], making this technique useful to detect various metal ions, nucleic acids, and proteins. ECL sensors usually consist of a working electrode (at which the ECL is mainly generated), a reference electrode, and a counter electrode. When the electrons of the working electrode move to the electrode surface, the electrons will be transferred to the ECL probe (GQDs), and the excited electrons will emit light when they return to the ground state. This is the working principle for GQDs-based ECL sensors. Table 5 summarizes the ECL-based sensing using GQDs.

Lu et al. developed a sensor for DNA damage detection by employing ECL resonance energy transfer (ERET) between GQDs and AuNPs (Fig. 11 a) [137]. In the typical procedure, AuNPs were first linked with a probe of single-stranded DNA (cp3 ssDNA) to form AuNPs-ssDNA. Through the ERET mechanism, the ECL of GQDs was quenched after the non-covalent binding of AuNPs-ssDNA to the GQDs, however, the ECL intensity of GQDs was enhanced when the AuNPs-ssDNA was hybridized with target p53 DNA to produce AuNPs-dsDNA. This ECL intensity enhancement was caused by the weakened non-covalent interaction between GQDs and dsDNA. As a result, the discrepancy between the quenching efficiencies of AuNPs-ssDNA and AuNP-dsDNA to GQDs could be compared to realize DNA damage identification (Fig. 11b), demonstrating a linear detection range from 25 – 400 nM with a LoD of 13 nM for target p53 ssDNA.

The first report on ECL-based sensing using GQDs was actually reported by Li et al. [138] in which they utilized cysteine as a masking agent and K2S2O8 as a coreactant to enable Cd2+ induced ECL quenching of GQDs in the range of 20 – 150 nM with a LoD of 13 nM. The following study conducted by Dong et al. involved a coreactant ECL system including GQDs and cysteine that could produce a strong cathodic ECL signal usable for Pb2+ detection [139]. It was pointed out that the thiol group was the key factor of the ECL reaction, and the following inferences were made. First, the thiol group was anodized first to generate RS·, and then reacted with oxygen in the water to generate RSO2·. Then, autocatalysis occurred, generating three different free radicals (RSO·, RSO2·, and RSO3·) and finally reacting with the GQD with an additional electron from the cathode to generate an excited GQD. The excited GQD would finally relax and produce an emission, which was the ECL. (Fig. 12 a). The developed ECL system was usable for Pb2+ detection with a wide linear range from 0.1 – 10 μM and LoD of 70 nM (Fig. 12b).

Based on the discussed PL, CL, and ECL methods for the detection of biological and environmental substances, they have their own advantages and disadvantages related to their own detection principles. Compared with PL which uses a light source as its excitation energy, CL and ECL have no interferences from the background light, inducing a wider detection range and more sensitivity. However, PL provides easier and simpler detection procedures than both CL and ECL. In addition, PL may also offer real-time monitoring not only for ex situ, but also in situ which may not be possible for CL and ECL. Hence, the best sensing technique among those three may strongly depend on the desired targeted analytes, wherein one targeted analyte may be more suitable for one technique but not for the others. For the readers’ convenience, we further summarized the advantages and disadvantages of these PL, CL, and ECL sensing techniques in Table 6 .

The outstanding properties of GQDs have captivated many people to delve deep into the exploration of suitable synthesis methods and advanced optical-based sensing applications. Coupled with the limitation of non-renewable energy resources, the development of more environmental-friendly synthesis methods of GQDs with controllable properties and high production yield from naturally available bioresources has been the forefront for scientists and researchers. Moreover, the ease of functionalization makes GQDs to be amenable for broad application scope, particularly for sensing applications to enhance the QY and/or add more active sites to improve the sensing performance and even enable the detection of analytes that have never been reported.

Nevertheless, the precise understanding of the luminescence properties of GQDs is still in an infant stage, despite its existence since 2010 [6]. One of the main reasons can be due to the structural heterogeneity of GQDs obtained from different synthesis methods, rendering an accurate and actual study on their properties to be challenging. Moreover, the synthesized GQDs from the same synthesis method using different precursors or vice versa may result in two GQDs having completely different properties, which further complicates the matter at hand. This will indeed affect the possible application offered by one GQD may not be possible for the others, as reflected by diverse sensing mechanisms proposed in the literature. DFT simulations have also been widely performed to provide more understanding and insight into the luminescence mechanism as they can isolate the role of certain functional groups, heteroatom dopants, and edges, yet the results obtained still lack supportive experimental data. There is an urgent need to develop synthesis methods capable of producing GQDs with well-defined structures or even precise engineering down to the atomic scale to achieve not only better application performances, but also more fundamental understanding of the GQDs properties.

Looking at it from another point of view, the above challenges certainly make the research about GQDs is not exhaustive, and should entice many scientists and researchers across many multidisciplinary natures to contribute to: (1) the fundamental understanding, (2) the development of environmental-friendly, facile, novel, and sustainable synthesis method with high production yield, as well as tunable- and controlled properties; (3) the development of optical-based sensors with greater sensitivity and selectivity, and even explore new sensing concepts which have never been realized by other materials, and (4) the combination of nanoscale GQDs with microscale or macroscale materials to form nanocomposites materials having multifunctional properties for more advanced and novel applications, including imaging, sensing, catalysis, drug delivery, optoelectronic devices, and energy-related applications. In these regards, one should expect many more exciting discoveries about GQDs to arise in the future even traversing the current state-of-the-art.
